narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 69.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows solid aspects, like coming from an upper-middle-class family background, being a white male planning full-time studies—traits that align closely with the majority of applicants—but it also highlights some key academic hurdles. Specifically, your Year 1 school decile ranking of 2 is well below the typical 5.71 average, placing you in the lower performance group early on. Similarly, your Year 3 decile of 2 lags behind the average of 5.53, signaling consistent challenges in coursework compared to peers. Your LSAT score of 31 falls short of the 36.7 average, which the model saw as a moderate drag on your predicted success. Rounding out the picture, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 is under the 3.21 norm, while your demographics didn't sway things much either way. In assessing your case, the model weighed your low Year 1 decile as the single biggest influence pushing toward a failure prediction, followed closely by your Year 3 decile, then your LSAT score, family income bracket, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status in that order of impact—all subtly tipping the scales against passing. None of these stood out as strongly protective factors; instead, your below-average academics across the board made the prediction lean negative when stacked against successful students. Typical admitted applicants have stronger deciles around the middle-to-upper range, higher LSATs near 37, and GPAs above 3.2, which correlate with first-time bar passage. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim above 36, build a stronger GPA through grad-level courses if possible, and target top-decile performance in any upcoming academic work to mimic high-achievers. With dedicated bar prep and these improvements, you could shift your profile toward the passing side many students occupy. Hang in there—this is a roadmap, not a dead end, and many have turned similar profiles around.
